coredata.dc:title: Carboxymethyl cellulose and sodium alginate-enhanced hydrogel for carbon dots loading: A novel platform for pH sensing and sensitive detection of Al3+ and Ag+
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Miaomiao
Zhou, Meng
Wang, Yunyun
Mao, Caihong
Pang, Shujie
Meng, Ting
Yang, Xudong
coredata.dc:description: To address the challenges associated with the storage and application of traditional carbon dot (CDs) solutions, this study introduces a cyan fluorescent carbon dot-based hydrogel (CDs-SCH). The hydrogel was synthesized by integrating cyan fluorescent CDs, derived from penicillamine and m-phenylenediamine, with carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) and sodium alginate (SA), which was then mixed with acrylamide (AM). The resulting CDs-SCH hydrogel was extensively characterized, focusing on its morphology, chemical structure, and fluorescence behavior. The fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel was enhanced by 3.23 times compared to the original CDs. The fluorescence response of the CDs-SCH hydrogel to pH variations was examined, demonstrating its capability to visually monitor the freshness of aquatic products such as fish and shrimp. Furthermore, Al3+ and Ag+ ions were found to significantly modulate the fluorescence, with Al3+ enhancing and Ag+ quenching the fluorescence, displaying reliable detection limits and linearity. The hydrogel's ability to detect glutathione (GSH) via Ag+ reduction to Ag was also explored. Additionally, the hydrogel exhibited stable Al3+ adsorption, with the process following pseudo-second-order kinetics and the Langmuir adsorption model. As a versatile and responsive material, the CDs-SCH hydrogel holds potential for applications in intelligent food packaging and environmental ion detection.

The sensing performance of different fluorescent sensors used for the detection of metal ions is reviewed.

head.ce:title: Carboxymethyl cellulose and sodium alginate-enhanced hydrogel for carbon dots loading: A novel platform for pH sensing and sensitive detection of Al3+ and Ag+

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the challenges associated with the storage and application of traditional carbon dot (CDs) solutions, this study introduces a cyan fluorescent carbon dot-based hydrogel (CDs-SCH). The hydrogel was synthesized by integrating cyan fluorescent CDs, derived from penicillamine and m-phenylenediamine, with carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) and sodium alginate (SA), which was then mixed with acrylamide (AM). The resulting CDs-SCH hydrogel was extensively characterized, focusing on its morphology, chemical structure, and fluorescence behavior. The fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel was enhanced by 3.23 times compared to the original CDs. The fluorescence response of the CDs-SCH hydrogel to pH variations was examined, demonstrating its capability to visually monitor the freshness of aquatic products such as fish and shrimp. Furthermore, Al3+ and Ag+ ions were found to significantly modulate the fluorescence, with Al3+ enhancing and Ag+ quenching the fluorescence, displaying reliable detection limits and linearity. The hydrogel's ability to detect glutathione (GSH) via Ag+ reduction to Ag was also explored. Additionally, the hydrogel exhibited stable Al3+ adsorption, with the process following pseudo-second-order kinetics and the Langmuir adsorption model. As a versatile and responsive material, the CDs-SCH hydrogel holds potential for applications in intelligent food packaging and environmental ion detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmentally responsive portable smart materials can provide feedback by sensing changes in the physical or chemical properties of the material triggered by changes in external environmental conditions [1–3]. Due to its rapid feedback and instrument-free mode of action, its development has received widespread attention. Especially in recent years, the frequent occurrence of environmental pollution problems has made the development and research of multi-functional portable smart response materials with low cost, high efficiency and environmental friendliness a focus of attention [2–4].

pH sensing materials for accurate pH monitoring have great potential for application in the fields of colorimetric sensing [5], chemical engineering [6], healthcare [7], food chemistry [8], and environmental monitoring [7,9]. So far, most pH sensing materials (e.g., organic fluorescent dyes [10–12], toxic semiconductor quantum dots [12]) have been limited in their wide applications due to insufficient photostability and complex synthesis. In contrast, fluorescent sensing materials have great potential for application due to their fast response, excellent stability, cost-effectiveness and simple operation. pH sensing materials with excellent optical stability and environmental compatibility are important for food freshness detection [13,14]. For fish and shrimp aquatic products, detection of spoilage requires the determination of volatile amines such as trimethylamine, dimethylamine and ammonia in the sample, and the extraction process is laborious and time-consuming [14,15]. Incorporating pH sensing materials into food packaging design and exploring non-leaching, simple, rapid and non-invasive assays for real-time assessment of aquatic product freshness to indicate food quality at the time of purchase or prior to consumption can better meet market demands.

Al3+ is a low-toxicity and non-essential metal, and its long-term accumulation in the human body can interfere with thinking and memory.Al3+ has been used as a flocculant in water treatment processes, and trace amounts of Al3+ can be found essentially in drinking water [16]. However, few studies have been reported on the safe and effective removal of Al3+. Similarly, Ag+ is genotoxic in mammalian cells in vitro [17]. There is a potential risk when Ag+ is used in food packaging or contact materials. There is an urgent need for effective materials to detect, adsorb and remove harmful metal ions from the environment. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of multifunctional carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials with high luminescence, chemical solubility, easy surface modification, biocompatibility, and low cost, have been recognised as very capable modes in the field of metal ion detection [18,19]. CDs with surface hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups are usually negatively charged, which can directly bind to positively charged metal ions through electrostatic and/or ligand interactions, and then cause shifts in emission intensity or fluorescence spectra [20]. However, as of now, most of the studies on CDs are limited to detection only, and there are few reports on the development of CDs capable of simultaneously detecting and removing metal ions in an environmentally friendly, effective, and economical manner. In addition, organic matter is usually present in the actual testing wastewater, which is prone to trigger fluorescence bursting (ACQ) caused by the aggregation of CDs [21]. Most CDs are soluble in water and difficult to remove from water, which can cause secondary contamination [22]. Due to the pathophysiological significance of metal ions, higher demands have been placed on methods for treating metal ions in biological systems, the environment, food and water. In summary, the study of multifunctional materials combining fluorescence detection and removal capabilities is of high research value.

Hydrogels are functional carriers [23], whose 3D polymer networks are formed by physical or chemical cross-linking to provide many binding sites for small molecules [24–26]. Compositing CDs with hydrogels not only solves the common ACQ phenomenon of CDs and ensures economic, rapid and real-time monitoring performance, but also brings structural diversity and achieves an effective combination of fluorescence detection and removal capabilities to broaden its application scope. Hydrogels can be prepared from natural or synthetic polymers. Natural polymer hydrogels are emerging as functionally diverse materials, which are more prominent due to their low cost, good biocompatibility and biodegradability. Natural polymer hydrogels are more valuable to study compared to synthetic polymer hydrogels in terms of source, technology and applicability [27]. Currently, various natural polymers have been used to construct CDs/natural polymer composite hydrogels, including chitosan, cellulose, agarose, and DNA [28]. Cellulose, as the most abundant natural polymer material on earth, has become very popular in its own research [29,30]. Research has attracted much attention in recent years in the fields of smart electronics [31], energy storage [32], electrical devices [33] and water treatment by virtue of its renewability, good mechanical strength, environmental friendliness, biocompatibility and biodegradability. There have been some recent reports on cellulose-based hydrogels. Lu et al. prepared inner ionic hydrogels with semi-interpenetrating networks using sodium carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) as a nanofiller, lithium chloride (LiCl) as an ion transport conductor and polyacrylamide (PAM) as a polymer matrix [34]. The polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) elastomer was then used to completely encapsulate the outer layer of the hydrogel to develop a novel skin-inspired hydrogel-elastomer blend with a sandwich structure and strong interfacial adhesion for mechano-thermal multimode sensing applications, developing a new material platform for monitoring human health in extreme environments. Sang et al. synthesized multifunctional conductive hydrogels with excellent strain/pressure sensing and photoresponsivity via hydrogen bonding interactions between 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-yloxy oxidised cellulose nanofibres (TOCNs) and poly(dopamine-modified) black phosphorus (BP@PDA) [35]. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) [36,37] is a naturally occurring polymer derived from cellulose by the introduction of carboxymethyl (-CH2COOH), and sodium alginate (SA) is a by-product of the extraction of iodine and mannitol from brown algae or seaweeds [38].The surfaces of the two cellulose feedstocks are rich in functional groups such as -OH and -COOH, which endow them with easy-to-modify hydrogen bond donor-acceptor properties. Therefore, we believe that the combination of cellulose-based hydrogels with CDs has great potential for development.

In this study, cyan fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using penicillamine and m-phenylenediamine as carbon sources. These were crosslinked with carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) and sodium alginate (SA) as precursors, and further mixed with acrylamide (AM) to form a biomass carbon dot-based fluorescent hydrogel with a three-dimensional network structure (CDs-SCH hydrogel) via thermally initiated free radical polymerization. The synthesis method is simple, environmentally friendly, and cost-effective. The resulting CDs-SCH hydrogel exhibits superior fluorescence properties and stability compared to the CDs alone. The fluorescence intensity and color changes of the hydrogel were investigated under varying pH conditions, demonstrating its potential for monitoring the freshness of aquatic products through visual color change. Intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) triggered by Al3+ and -COOH groups in CDs-SCH hydrogels, and fluorescence enhancement of the latter was observed, enabling specific detection of Al3+. Due to the fact that Ag+ belongs to thiophilic metal ions and the complexation of -NH2 and -COOH with Ag+ on the surface of CDs-SCH hydrogels, Ag+ can effectively burst the fluorescence of CDs-SCH hydrogels. GSH, with its strong nucleophilicity, is able to compete for the capture of Ag+ in the CDs-SCH hydrogel/Ag+ system to achieve the effective fluorescence of the Ag+-burst. The CDs-SCH hydrogel/Ag+ system was used indirectly for GSH detection based on Ag+-burst fluorescence reduction. Adsorption experiments confirmed that the CDs-SCH hydrogel showed excellent Al3+ adsorption performance, exceeding 152.84 mg/g. The adsorption process conformed to the proposed second-order model and Langmuir model, respectively. The CDs-SCH hydrogel has potential applications as an effective portable platform.

D-Penicillamine (C5H11NO2S, 98.00 %) was purchased from Jilin Zhongxin Research and Extension Technology Co., Ltd. and m-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2, 99.00 %) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Institute of Fine Chemicals. Potassium persulfate (KPS, 99.99 %), acrylamide (AM, 99.00 %), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl- (3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) were purchased from Aladdin Industries. Sodium alginate (SA), sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (Na-CMC, sodium content (6.50–8.50 %)) were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory. N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA) was purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group, Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The microstructure and size distribution of CDs were studied using a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA). The internal structure of CDs was studied using a SmartLab X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan). The elements in the CDs were analyzed using a JSM-5610LV X-ray spectrometer (Electron, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured using a Cary 5000 ultraviolet-visible near-infrared (UV-VisNIR) spectrometer (Agilent, USA). Surface functional groups were analyzed using a Nicolet IS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) was used for photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) analysis. The static water contact angle (WCA) of the hydrogels was measured by a contact angle analyzer (Jy-Phb). About 10 μL of water was dropped on the surface of each hydrogel and the contact angle was measured.

1 mmol of m-phenylenediamine, 0.5 mmol of D-penicillamine were dissolved ultrasonically in 25 mL of ultrapure water and the mixture was placed in a PTFE-lined reactor (50 mL) at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, it was cooled naturally to room temperature and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove the large agglomerated particles to obtain a brownish-yellow solution. The solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the agglomerated particles and dialyzed using dialysis tubing with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 100 Da for 24 h. During the dialysis process, the ultrapure water was changed every 3 h. The solution was purified with a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the agglomerated particles. The purified CDs solution was partially stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for backup. Solid CDs powder was obtained after vacuum drying.

Novel hydrogels were synthesized using thermally initiated free radical polymerization method. A 2 wt% CMC solution was configured and an equal mass of 2 wt% SA solution was added to the CMC solution to form a mixture. CDs, NHS, and EDC were added to the mixture and stirred at room temperature for 10 h under light protection to form CDs-SC solution. Add 7 g AM, 0.05 g MBA, 0.06 g KPS and stir at room temperature until the solid is completely dissolved, mix uniformly and introduce into the mold. Polymerization was carried out at 50 °C for 12 h. The prepared hydrogels were rinsed and soaked in ultrapure water to eliminate residual reagents to obtain CDs-SCH hydrogel. Hydrogels configured using different concentrations of CDs (0.50 mg/mL, 1.00 mg/mL, 1.50 mg/mL, 2.00 mg/mL, 2.50 mg/mL) solutions were named 1CDs-SCH, 2CDs-SCH, 3CDs-SCH, 4CDs-SCH, and 5CDs-SCH, respectively. Hydrogel without CDs was named 0CDs-SCH.

Fig. 10 depicts the synthesis of CDs-SCH hydrogel. Firstly, CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using m-phenylenediamine and penicillamine as raw materials at 200 °C for 6 h. CDs-SC mixture was synthesized by auxiliary graft copolymerization of CDs onto CMC and SA using CMC and SA as raw materials with the addition of EDC and NHS, under the condition of sufficient reaction under the protection from light; finally, CDs-SCH hydrogel was synthesized by thermally initiated free radical polymerization by adding the monomer AM, the initiator KPS, and the cross-linking agent MBA, and then synthesized by thermally initiated free radical polymerization at 50 °C for 12 h. CDs-SCH hydrogel was synthesized by thermally initiated free radical polymerization at 50 °C for 12 h.

The tensile properties of hydrogels were evaluated using an AGS-X universal testing machine (Shimadzu, Japan). Dumbbell-shaped hydrogels of 30 mm in length, 2 mm in width, and 3 mm in thickness were prepared, and the samples were tested at a tensile speed of 100 mm/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-SCH hydrogel for seafood preservation monitoring packaging materials
ce:section.ce:para: Freshness of shrimp and fish was monitored using CDs-SCH hydrogel. Hydrogel strips 3 cm long, 1 cm wide and 0.3 cm thick were made and fresh shrimp or fish of similar weight were placed in clean and dry petri dishes. Fresh shrimp or fish of similar weight were placed in clean and dry petri dishes. The strips were placed next to each other, and the petri dishes were sealed with sealing film and left open, respectively. Changes in the appearance of the hydrogels were recorded in 6 h cycles.

In addition, the effect of CDs-SCH hydrogels to retard shrimp deterioration was explored. Hydrogel gel strips of 1 cm width and 3 cm length of 0.1 cm thickness were fabricated. Fresh shrimps were purchased from the market and the shrimps were fully packed and half covered with hydrogel film and stored in dry Picasso Petri dishes, which were sealed with a sealing film. Changes in the shrimp were recorded every 6 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of Al3+ and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: The Al3+ and Ag + selectivity of CDs-SCH hydrogel with respect to other metal ions was studied using the following metal ions: Al3+, Ag+, Ca2+, K+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Na+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Zn2+ and Fe3+. Standard ion solutions were prepared at a concentration of 0.1 M to investigate the fluorescence intensity changes of CDs and CDs-SCH hydrogels. At room temperature, 100 μL of CDs solution with CDs content of 1.50 mg/mL and hydrogel strips with thickness of 0.3 cm, length of 3 cm and width of 1 cm were incubated in 3 mL of different metal ions for 5 min, and the fluorescence curves were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. The visually produced color changes were observed under natural and 365 nm UV light irradiation and photographed for storage.

The response of CDs-SCH hydrogel to anions, amines and carbohydrates was investigated. Using OH-, NO2 −, NO3 −, H2PO4 −, HSO3 −, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, Br−, Cl−, S2−, F−, PO4 3−, B2O7 2−, MnO4 −, SiO3 2−, histidine (His), cysteine (Cys), alanine (Ala), ascorbic acid (GSH), and glutathione (GSH). Glutathione (GSH), Dopamine (DA) Citric acid (CA), Sucrose (Suc), Glucose (Glu) and Fructose (Fru), standard solutions were prepared at a concentration of 0.1 M. 100 μL of CDs solution with a CDs content of 1.50 mg/mL and hydrogel strips with a thickness of 0.3 cm, a length of 3 cm and a width of 1 cm were added to 3 mL of the standard solution and allowed to react for 5 min at a stable room temperature. Subsequently, the change of fluorescence curve at 390 nm excitation wavelength was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity detection of Al3+、Ag+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of Al3+ and Ag+ solutions (0–1000 μM) were pre-prepared to test the sensitivity of CDs-SCH hydrogel to Al3+ and Ag+. Hydrogel strips of 0.3 cm in thickness, 3 cm in length and 1 cm in width with a CDs content of 1.50 mg/mL were immersed in 3 mL of different concentrations of Al3+ or Ag+ solutions, and the fluorescence spectra were measured by incubation for 5 min at room temperature.

To detect GSH, hydrogel strips of 0.3 cm in thickness, 3 cm in length and 1 cm in width with a CDs content of 1.50 mg/mL were added to 3 mL Ag+ (1000 μM) solution at room temperature, and the strips were fished out after 5 min to obtain the CDs-SCH hydrogel/Ag+ system, and the optimal fluorescence intensity was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. Configuration of GSH different concentration solutions (0–1000 μM). The CDs-SCH hydrogel/Ag+ system was added to 3 mL of different concentrations of GSH solutions and their fluorescence spectra were collected at room temperature.

A certain volume of aluminum standard solution was accurately pipetted and proportionally diluted with 0.05 mol/L NaOH solution to formulate aluminum standard working solution with mass concentrations of 0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80 and 100 mg/L. The solution was diluted with 0.05 mol/L NaOH solution to form aluminum standard working solution. Pipette 0.5 mL of aluminum standard working solution in a 10 mL cuvette, add 1 mL of mulberry pigment ethanol solution (100 μg/mL), dropwise add glacial acetic acid to the formed acetic acid - sodium acetate buffer solution pH 4, add water to the scale, shake well. Let it stand for 5 min, measure the fluorescence intensity of 390 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption capacity of Al3+ by CDs-SCH hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
The CDs-SCH samples were cut into discs with a diameter of 1 cm and a thickness of 0.3 cm, and the mass of each disc was measured. The discs were placed in Al3+ ion solution, 100 mL of a certain mass concentration of Al3+ solution and 0.5 g of aqueous gel were added into a 250 mL conical flask sequentially, and the rotational speed was set at 180 r/min and the temperature at 25 °C to make the reaction in a water-bath constant-temperature oscillator for a number of After several hours, the supernatant in the conical flask was aspirated, and the mass concentration of Al3+ in the supernatant was measured by mulberry pigment complexation fluorescence spectrophotometry. All adsorption experiments were repeated three times. The equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe) was calculated using the following equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and CDs-SCH hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The structures of CDs and CDs-SCH hydrogel were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy spectrometry (EDS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The morphological structure of CDs was investigated by TEM (Fig. 1a), and it was clearly observed that the synthesized CDs were spherical and well dispersed. The size of the CDs was calculated to be 7.5 ± 1.0 nm based on the Gaussian fitting curve in the inset of Fig. 1a. SEM was used to determine the morphology of the CDs and the hydrogels (Fig. 1b-c). The SCH hydrogels exhibited 3D porous network structures. These 3D network structures can increase the ion migration rate and enhance the adsorption capacity of the materials [39,40]. Compared with SCH hydrogel, CDs-SCH hydrogel have a denser structure. This is mainly due to the hydrogen bonding between the CDs and the molecular chains of the hydrogel, suggesting that the doping of CDs into the hydrogel alters its internal structure. XRD analysis was performed to determine the crystallinity of the CDs (Fig. 1d), and the broadened peak at 22.58 indicates that the synthesized CDs exhibit a typical amorphous carbon structure [41]. The elemental composition of CDs was analyzed using EDS (Fig. S1). The results showed that C, N, S and O were uniformly distributed in the CDs. The chemical structures of CDs-SCH hydrogel, SCH hydrogel, and CDs were analyzed by FT-IR to investigate the interactions between the fluorescent hydrogel components as shown in Fig. S1e. The absorption bands at 2932 cm−1 and 3413 cm−1 were attributed to the main absorption bands of the OH stretching vibration overlapping with that of the NH stretching vibration overlapping [42]. The peak at 1317 cm−1 is attributed to the CH stretching vibration of the aromatic ring. The band at 1403 cm−1 is related to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of -COO- [43,44].1348 and 1450 cm−1 have been assigned to C-NO and C-NH, respectively [45].1653 cm−1 shows the main absorption band of CO-NH. The band at 1155 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibrations of the CN, CS, and CO bonds [46], and the band at 2364 cm−1 is shown to be the main absorption band of the SH stretching vibration [47]. The successful doping of S atoms in CDs was confirmed.

The hydrophilicity of the hydrogel can be verified by measuring the water contact angle, as shown in Fig. S2, the water contact angle of the SCH hydrogel is about 55–59°, and the water contact angle of the CDs-doped CDs-SCH hydrogel is between 62 and 71°. Although the water contact angle of the CDs-SCH hydrogel is slightly bigger, the water contact angle is less than 90° in general, and therefore, the prepared fluorescent hydrogels have a hydrophilic [48]. The elemental composition and chemical state of the CDs-SCH hydrogels were analyzed using XPS. The total XPS spectrum of the CDs-SCH hydrogels is shown in Fig. 1g, showing four typical peaks C1s (288 eV), N1s (400 eV), O1s (531 eV) and S2p (168 eV) [49]. The high-resolution spectra of these four elements were then processed by peak fitting. The C1 s spectra at 284.8 eV (C=C/C-C), 287.9 eV (C-C=O), 285.8 eV (CO) and 283.9 eV (C-N/C-O) showed four bands, as shown in Fig. 1h [50]. The fitting curves of the N1s peaks are shown in Fig. S2a, with three peaks at 399.4 eV (Pyridine N), 400 eV (Amino N), and 401.1 eV (Graphite N), matching Pyridine N, Amino N, and Graphite N, respectively [51]. Fig. S2b O1s spectrum is divided into two peaks, 531.2 eV (C- = O) and 532.6 eV (C=O) [52]. As shown in Fig. 1i, one peak of SC is present in the XPS spectrum of S2p, and the binding 2p3/2 of S is 168.2 eV [53]. These XPS results are in agreement with the FT-IR analysis. The XRD, FT-IR and XPS analyses indicate that the preparation of CDs-SCH hydrogels was successful.

Good mechanical properties are crucial for hydrogel applications [54]. We explored the optimal CDs doping content by adding different amounts of CDs to SCH hydrogels, performing tensile test analysis (Fig. 2a) and evaluating elastic modulus and toughness (Fig. 2b). Notably, hydrogels containing different amounts of CDs exhibited different degrees of tensile strength and strain compared to SCH hydrogels without CDs. In the absence of CDs, the fracture stress of the hydrogel was 73.53 kPa and the strain was 812.24 %. With the increase of CDs content, the fracture stress and strain of the hydrogel were gradually enhanced, and the fracture stress of the hydrogel increased to 546.95 kPa and the strain was 3149.71 % at its content of 1.50 mg/mL, and then both showed a decreasing trend. Notably, as shown in Fig. 2b, the trend of hydrogel fracture toughness was the same as that of strain with the increase of CDs content. When the content of CDs was 1.50 mg/mL, the fracture toughness reached 1069.94 kJ/m3, indicating that the toughness of hydrogel was greatly improved. The improvement of the tensile mechanical properties of hydrogels may be attributed to the rich hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs which can form a network structure of hydrogen-bonded cross-links with the side groups of the CMC and SA molecular chains, which can lead to the enhancement of the cohesive energy density of the materials [55].

The strain-stress curves (Fig. 2c) and corresponding elastic moduli (Fig. 2d) of CDs-SCH hydrogel were obtained by continuous tensile tests to investigate the fatigue resistance of CDs-SCH hydrogel. When under 100 % deformation condition and cyclic stretching for 10 times, an obvious lag loop can be observed during the first loading-unloading cycle, but the other nine loading-unloading cycle curves are almost identical. This is because there is an inhomogeneous structure inside the hydrogel, and the first loading-unloading cycle stabilizes the structure uniformly. Thereby, the nine loading-unloading curves almost overlapped. The stress level of CDs-SCH hydrogel was kept above 93.59 %, which confirms that the hydrogel has good fatigue resistance [56]. In addition, a series of tensile, twisting and loading experiments (Fig. 2e-f) were performed, which likewise demonstrated the excellent mechanical properties of the hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of CDs and CDs-SCH hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were studied to confirm the fluorescence properties of CDs and CDs-SCH hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 3i, the CDs were well dispersed in water, yellow in natural light, and exhibited strong cyan fluorescence under 365 nm irradiation. Fig. 3a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs in aqueous media. The absorption spectra of CDs show the presence of an absorption band at 284 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* jump of the sp2 aromatic carbon [57]. Notably, this band was also observed in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs-SCH hydrogel (Fig. 3d), indicating that they have similar UV properties.

The excitation and emission spectra of the prepared CDs were measured. As shown in Fig. 3a, the spectra roughly show asymmetric narrow bands. When the sample is excited by 390 nm, the CDs can emit cyan light centered at 500 nm. It is consistent with the emission color of CDs in solution under UV (365 nm) irradiation. The same conclusion is reached by the CIE chromaticity diagram in Fig. 3h. The quantum yield of CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as a standard. The sample showed a high quantum yield of 29.7 %. To explore the luminescence properties, a series of emission spectra were measured at different excitation wavelengths from 360 nm to 420 nm (Fig. 3b), and the CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission peak shifts from 460 nm to 528 nm. This is due to the fact that the surface of CDs is enriched with a variety of functional groups such as various -OH, -COOH, -NH2, etc., which make the surface states of the CDs extremely complex, and the formation of multiple energy levels between the energy gaps of the surface states corresponds to multiple electron leaps, resulting in excitation-dependent emission from the CDs. In addition, it has been shown that the emission may originate from two possible prominent electron leaps, C-band and S-band leaps [58]. In the CDs emission wavelength-dependent excitation spectra (Fig. 3c), the peak in the 350 nm range corresponds to the C-band and the 390 nm range to the S-band [59]. As the monitored wavelength increases, the weakening of the C-band intensity is accompanied by an enhancement of the S-band intensity, and the 500 nm excitation spectrum has the highest S-band intensity, corresponding to the optimal emission under 390 nm excitation in Fig. 3e. The C-band and S-bands exhibit redshifts, which implies that both contribute to the emission leading to the excitation-dependent emission of CDs.

As shown in Fig. 3i, cyan fluorescence photographs of CDs-SCH hydrogel were recorded under UV excitation light. Reference to quinine sulphate as a standard showed a quantum yield of 34.8 % for CDs-SCH hydrogels. The improved quantum yield of CDs-SCH hydrogels is due to the prevention of collisions and aggregation between CDs during gelation [60,61]. The corresponding normalized spectra of CDs-SCH hydrogel are shown in Fig. 3e. CDs-SCH hydrogel still has luminescent behaviors related to the excitation wavelength, and their emission wavelengths are also red-shifted with the increase of the excitation wavelength. The general trend of the emission peaks of all the spectra did not change much, but the peak intensities were enhanced overall. With the increase of the excitation wavelength, the CDs-SCH hydrogel showed the highest emission peak intensity near 500 nm, with the peak intensity 3.23 times that of CDs. The excitation spectra related to the emission wavelength of CDs-SCH hydrogel were observed (Fig. 3f). The C-band and S-band contribute equally to the emission, but the S-band exhibits higher peaks compared to CDs, which may be attributed to the difference in the aggregation form of CDs in the hydrogel-like and solution states, resulting in a substantial impact on the surface state. The cross-complexation and electron transfer between CDs, lead to a significant increase in the emission intensity between the solid CDs and the solution-state CDs emission differences. A 3D fluorescence topography of the CDs-SCH hydrogel was recorded, as shown in Fig. 3i. The CDs-SCH hydrogel shows a vertically elongated profile at the emission maximum of 390 nm, corresponding to the C-band and S-band jumps at the emission maximum of 390 nm. In addition, a small extension towards the UV region is observed in the emission band.

Steady-state photoluminescence was performed on CDs-SCH hydrogel under external stimuli such as temperature and light. The change of fluorescence intensity with temperature (λex = 390 nm) was studied by water bath method. As shown in Fig. S4a, the temperature of the CDs-SCH hydrogel was controlled between 40°-80°, and the fluorescence of the hydrogel did not decrease significantly with increasing temperature, indicating that the temperature did not have a significant effect on the fluorescence properties of the prepared CDs-SCH hydrogel. NaCl salt solution with concentration of 0–0.5 M was added to the CDs-SCH hydrogel, and salt tolerance test was carried out after standing for 5 min, as shown in Fig. S4b, the stability of fluorescence intensity of CDs-SCH hydrogel at the optimal excitation wavelength was better in the range of the salt solution concentration tested. The changes in fluorescence intensity of CDs-SCH hydrogel were measured over a period of 10 days. Fig. S4c shows that CDs-SCH hydrogel exhibits good stability. The CDs-SCH hydrogel was irradiated under 365 nm UV light for 60 min, and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-SCH hydrogel at the optimal excitation wavelength was recorded every 10 min. As shown in Fig. S4d, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-SCH hydrogel remained unchanged, indicating its good light resistance and stable fluorescence properties under UV irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-responsive luminescent behavior of CDs-SCH hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence changes of CDs-SCH hydrogels in different pH buffers were investigated, as shown in Fig. 4a. The original peak position of CDs-SCH hydrogel under 390 nm excitation had a significant shift in the buffer solution, with a significant red-shift in acidic environment, and the peak was shifted to 509 nm when the pH value reached 1.0. The blue shift is obvious in alkaline environment, with the peak at 487 nm at pH 13.0. The emission peak is completely burst at pH 14.0. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-SCH hydrogel was significantly enhanced with decreasing pH under acidic conditions, and the fluorescence of CDs-SCH hydrogel was attenuated with increasing pH under alkaline conditions. The change in the intensity of the fluorescence peaks of CDs-SCH hydrogel at different pH was attributed to the protonation/deprotonation of the oxygen-containing functional groups, such as -OH, -COOH, etc. [62,63]. In addition to the changes in the emission peaks' position and intensity changes, the fluorescence color of CDs-SCH hydrogel also changed with acidic and basic conditions, which indicated that CDs-SCH hydrogel could achieve quantitative and qualitative detection of pH changes. When the pH range varied from 6.0 to 1.0, the cyan color of the CDs-SCH hydrogel was subsequently increased. When the pH range was from 8.0 to 14.0, the color of CDs-SCH hydrogel gradually changed from cyan to transparent, See Fig. 4e. The above phenomena indicate that CDs-SCH hydrogel has potential application in the visual detection of wastewater pH.

The zeta potential and UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs-SCH hydrogel at different pH were investigated to further study the mechanism of action. As shown in Fig. 4b, the zeta potential of CDs-SCH hydrogel was tested at pH 2.0, 7.0 and 12.0, and the potential was positive at pH 7.0 (3.42 mV), remained positive at pH 2.0 (10.56 mV), and remained negative at pH 12.0 (−8.96 mV). The absorption spectra of CDs-SCH hydrogel at different pH values are shown in Fig. 4c. When the pH is less than 7.0, the absorption peaks are gradually enhanced with the increase of acidity. When the pH is 7.0–9.0, there is only a gradual weakening of the absorption intensity and no new absorption peaks appear. When in strong alkalinity (pH greater than 12.0), the absorption peak of CDs-SCH hydrogel gradually disappeared, and a new absorption peak appeared at 410 nm, probably because CDs-SCH hydrogel was deprotonated under strong alkaline conditions, the surface was negatively charged, and it gradually dissociated into non-fluorescent substances, and the -OH and -NH2 on the surface of CDs-SCH hydrogel after complete dissociation of the After the complete dissociation of -OH and -NH2 on the surface of CDs-SCH hydrogel, the products interact with a large amount of OH− in the solution to generate new products, resulting in a new absorption [13,64,65]. The above absorption spectra results are consistent with the changes in fluorescence color and intensity of CDs-SCH hydrogel at different pH.

The fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) is the ratio of the optimal emission peaks of the CDs-SCH hydrogel at different pH buffers to the optimal emission peaks at aqueous solution. Two linear curves can be fitted to F/F0 versus pH (Fig. 4d), indicating that CDs-SCH hydrogels can be used for quantitative detection of pH change. The value of F/F0 increases linearly with increasing H+ concentration in the pH range from 6.0 to 1.0, which can be described as: y = −0.25014× + 2.61080, R2 = 0.96725, and the LOD is 0.213 μM. When the pH value is 1.0, the F/F0 reaches 2.58. when the pH range varies from 8.0 to 14.0, the CDs-SCH hydrogel showed an excellent linear relationship between the bursting efficiency and OH− concentration, which can be described as: y = −0.13580× + 2.02569, R2 = 0.96256, and limit of detection (LOD) was 0.261 μM. When pH was 14.0, F/F0 was 0.03. The reproducibility of pH measurements was investigated by performing several cycles from pH 4.0 to 7.0 to 11.0 and recovering the pH using concentrated NaOH and HCl in the same solution of the CDs-SCH hydrogel. No pH drift was observed even after 5 cycles (Fig. S5). This experiment demonstrated that the response of CDs-SCH hydrogels to pH was fully reproducible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs-SCH hydrogel in freshness monitoring of aquatic products
ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia and various volatile amines are produced during the storage of shrimp, fish and other products, which raise the pH of the surrounding environment, especially in hermetically sealed packages where air exchange is limited by the environment [66]. Therefore, it is possible to monitor the deterioration process of shrimp and fish by observing changes in the appearance and color of the CDs-SCH hydrogel as well as the fluorescence under UV light. Fig. 5a show the color change of CDs-SCH hydrogel every 6 h during the deterioration process of shrimp and fish (36 h, room temperature 28 °C in summer). The color of the CDs-SCH hydrogel changed significantly over time, whether the CDs-SCH hydrogel was sealed with shrimp or fish or left open. In the shrimp deterioration monitoring group, the shrimp in the closed group started to show a distinct orange-red color from the 6 h, and rotting had already appeared at the 12 h. The contact portion of the CDs-SCH hydrogel shifted from a greenish color to a light greenish color. At the 24 h, the odor of shrimp decomposition intensified, and the CDs-SCH hydrogel turned blue under UV light. In contrast, the first 6 h of shrimp in the open group only slightly appeared orange-red, CDs-SCH hydrogel in the UV light color did not change significantly, and the 18 h began to rot, CDs-SCH hydrogel from cyan to light cyan. In the fish deterioration monitoring group, the fish in the closed group started to release yellow liquid at the 6 h, decayed and emitted a strong fishy odor, but it was not until the 18 h that the CDs-SCH hydrogel was exposed to the yellow liquid released by the fish that the CDs-SCH hydrogel changed from cyan to light cyan, and the CDs-SCH hydrogel turned blue gradually under the immersion of the yellow liquid from the 18 to 36 h. The overall decay of fish in the open group was not as high as that in the open group, and there was no significant change in the color of the fish under UV light. The overall decay of fish in the open group was not as high as that in the closed group, but the CDs-SCH hydrogel showed roughly the same color change under UV light, probably due to the limited overall production of trimethylamine by the fish during the decay process. In addition, the effect of CDs-SCH hydrogel on delaying the deterioration of shrimp was also explored, and the results are shown in Fig. 5b. It can be seen that the shrimp not encapsulated by CDs-SCH hydrogel had a distinct orange-red color after 6 h of placement. The rancid smell could be smelled at the 12 h, and the rotting deterioration was obvious. At 12 h, the freshness of shrimps wrapped with CDs-SCH hydrogels remained almost unchanged, showing only a slight reddening, and a slight odor was released only after 24 h. In conclusion, CDs-SCH hydrogel has a certain resistance to deterioration. This was attributed to its good UV and antioxidant properties, which delayed the spoilage of food products to a certain extent [1].

The fluorescence spectra of CDs-SCH hydrogel treated with different metal cations (e.g., Na+, K+, Ca2+) (Fig. 6a-b) and metal anions (e.g., OH−, NO3 −, CO3 2−), amino acids (e.g., Lys, Trp, Tyr) and carbohydrates (Glu, Fru, Suc) (Fig. S6a) under the same conditions were recorded. As shown in Fig. 6a-b, at the same concentration of 0.1 M, the fluorescence of CDs-SCH hydrogel was significantly enhanced when doped with Al3+, and the fluorescence was dramatically burst when doped with Ag+, while the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent hydrogels did not change much when doped with other common metal ions. Interestingly, the CDs-SCH hydrogel was more responsive to Al3+ and Ag+ relative to CDs.

The physical diagrams of CDs-SCH hydrogel treated with different metal cations under natural light and UV light are shown in Fig. 7 . The addition of Al3+ changed the color of CDs-SCH hydrogel from cyan to lime green under UV light. To explain this change, the UV–Vis and FT-IR spectra of CDs-SCH hydrogel were tested. Fig. S6b reveals a slight change in the UV–visible spectrum of CDs-SCH hydrogel in the region of 260–320 nm after the addition of Al3+. The absorption in the spectra was enhanced, but no new peaks appeared, just a simple superposition of their spectra. This result indicates that the reaction between CDs-SCH hydrogel and Al3+ did not produce new substances. The FT-IR spectra of the CDs-SCH hydrogel before and after the addition of Al3+ are shown in Fig. Sc. There was no disappearance of the original absorption bands and the appearance of new absorption peaks. This is consistent with the UV results. Observing the fluorescence emission profile of CDs-SCH hydrogel (Fig. 6b), the fluorescence of CDs-SCH hydrogel doped with Al3+ was significantly enhanced, and the optimal emission wavelength was shifted from 500 nm to 510 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was 2.86 times higher than that before the addition of Al3+. This was attributed to the electron transfer between Al3+ and carbonyl group. Therefore, we hypothesized that the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement is intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [36,67,68]. In addition, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of the CDs-SCH hydrogel was significantly enhanced with respect to CDs, which is a good indication of the significant improvement in the detection ability of this hydrogel.

After the addition of Ag+, the color of CDs-SCH hydrogel changed from cyan to colorless under UV light (Fig. 7), and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) decreased from 20.08 % to 14.64 % relative to CDs (Fig. 6b), and the degree of fluorescence burst was significantly enhanced, which once again confirms that doping of the gels can greatly improve the ion detection ability. The introduction of Ag+ significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity, probably due to the fact that Ag+ has a high-affinity preference for the thiols with a high-affinity preference, which is a thiophilic metal ion [69]. Meanwhile, CDs-SCH hydrogel has a large amount of -NH2 and -COOH on the surface, which can be complex with Ag+. Combined with the UV–vis spectra (Fig. S6b), a new weak absorption peak at 328 nm appeared due to the NAg ligand-triggered metal charge transfer. Indicating the possible formation of a stable complex between the CDs-SCH hydrogel and Ag+, which further led to the bursting of the fluorescence intensity in a ratiometric manner. As shown in Fig. S6d, the absorption spectrum of Ag+ did not overlap with the fluorescence emission spectrum or excitation spectrum of CDs, which excludes the possibility of a mechanistic role of the internal filtering effect and aids in corroborating the above mechanistic explanation.

In order to investigate the fluorescence enhancement of CDs-SCH hydrogels by Al3+ and the effective bursting of CDs-SCH hydrogel fluorescence by Ag+, the fluorescence intensities were obtained at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm by adding the same concentration of equal amounts of Al3+ and Ag+, respectively, to the fluorescent hydrogels containing other metal ions (0.1 M) (Fig. S6e-f). The relative fluorescence intensities of the hydrogels showed different degrees of enhancement and burst, respectively, indicating that the fluorescent hydrogels did not interfere with the detection of Al3+ and Ag+ in the presence of other metal ions coexisting with them. It is further shown that the fluorescent hydrogel has high specificity and excellent detection performance for Al3+ and Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of Al3+ and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence enhancement effect of Al3+ on CDs-SCH hydrogels was explored. As shown in Fig. 6c, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-SCH hydrogel was sensitively and proportionally enhanced with increasing Al3+ concentration. As shown in Fig. S7a, when the concentration of Al3+ was from 0 μM to 1000 μM, the color of CDs-SCH hydrogel gradually changed to yellow under natural light, and the fluorescence color changed from cyan to lime green under UV light (365 nm). The fluorescent hydrogel had no significant change in color under natural light and UV light after 5 h. The fluorescence color change of CDs-SCH hydrogel can be directly used for the visual detection of Al3+ by the naked eye. The system showed good linearity in the range of 0 to 1000 μM, y = 0.01494× + 0.93044, with an R2 value of 0.99368 and LOD = 0.31 μM, indicating that the CDs-SCH hydrogel can have high detection sensitivity for low concentration Al3+ solutions.

The degree of fluorescence bursting of CDs-SCH hydrogel by different concentrations of Ag+ was explored. As shown in Fig. 6e, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-SCH hydrogel decreased proportionally with increasing Ag+ concentration. As shown in Fig. S7b, the fluorescent hydrogel changed from slightly cyan to colorless with the enhancement of Ag+ concentration. There was no significant change in color under natural light and UV lamp after 5 h. The fluorescence color change of CDs-SCH hydrogel can be directly used for visual detection by the naked eye. The system showed good linearity in the range of 0–1000 μM, y = −0.00430× + 1.02178, R2=0.99241, LOD = 0.27 μM, suggesting that the CDs-SCH hydrogel has the potential to be used as a fluorescent sensor for Ag+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs-SCH hydrogel/Ag+ system to GSH
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-SCH hydrogel can be used as visual “on-off” fluorescence sensors, accompanied by a reversible switch from colorless to cyan fluorescence (Fig. 8a). Since GSH is extremely nucleophilic, it binds tightly to Ag+ [70,71]. When the CDs-SCH hydrogel/Ag+ system reacts with GSH, Ag+ in the CDs-SCH hydrogel is competitively trapped by GSH as the concentration of GSH increases, thus restoring most of the fluorescence (Fig. 8b).

The CDs-SCH hydrogel/Ag+ system (containing 0.1 M Ag+) was added to investigate the sensitivity of the “switching” fluorescence sensor. The fluorescence of the CDs-SCH hydrogel was burst by about 14.64 % in the presence of Ag+ (1000 μm), and an equivalent amount of GSH was required to restore most of the fluorescence of the CDs-SCH hydrogel with only 800 μM Ag+ (Fig. 8c). Most of the fluorescence of CDs-SCH hydrogel (Fig. 8c). In addition, the plot of relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) versus GSH concentration showed good linearity in the concentration range of 0–100 μM, y = 0.30669× + 0.00428, R2 = 0.99422, and LOD = 0.38 μM. These results indicate that the CDs-SCH hydrogel/Ag+ system has the potential to act as a fluorescent “fluorescence” system in the detection of GSH. The results suggest that the CDs-SCH hydrogel/Ag+ system has the potential as a fluorescent “switch” sensor for GSH detection.

To investigate the selectivity of CDs-SCH hydrogel for GSH, 14 possible interferents at a concentration of 0.1 μM were selected in this study and the changes in fluorescence intensity of CDs-SCH hydrogel were observed after the addition of interferents/Ag+ solution. The results showed (Fig. 8e) that AA, GSH, Cys, CA contain reducing groups such as carboxyl groups and thiols [72,73], which lead to a small conversion of Ag+ to Ag [71]. Apart from this, cations, carbohydrates, and the rest of the amino acids did not interfere with the reaction of Ag+ with CDs-SCH hydrogel, which caused the fluorescence of CDs-SCH hydrogel to burst.

Fig. 9a shows the adsorption amounts of CDs-SCH hydrogel and SCH hydrogel with time. The actual adsorption experiments of CDs-SCH hydrogel on Al3+ showed that the adsorption equilibrium of Al3+ on hydrogel about 210 min. Both hydrogels showed that the adsorption amount increased with the increase of the contact time, and the adsorption amount of CDs-SCH hydrogel was higher than that of SCH hydrogel. The adsorption kinetic module of the hydrogels was investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 9b-c. Comparing and analysing the correlation coefficients of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order adsorption models, the correlation coefficients calculated by the pseudo-second-order kinetic model were more suitable, which led to the inference that the kinetic adsorption mechanism of Al3+ by CDs-SCH hydrogels belonged to chemisorption.

The relationship between the adsorption of hydrogels and Al3+ concentration was investigated. As shown in Fig. 9d, the adsorption amounts of both CDs-SCH hydrogel and SCH hydrogels increased with the increase of initial concentration. In addition, the adsorption amount of CDs-SCH hydrogel was higher than that of SCH hydrogel at the same initial concentration, indicating that the addition of CDs to the hydrogel promoted the adsorption of Al3+. Adsorption isotherms of CDs-SCH hydrogels were investigated using Langmuir and Freundlich models (Fig. 7e-f). Among them, the Langmuir isotherm model is considered for monomolecular adsorption [74,75]. As can be seen from the relational equation in the figure, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model is more acceptable for the adsorption isotherm of Al3+, and the maximum adsorption of Al3+ on CDs-SCH hydrogel was 152.84 mg/g, which indicated that the adsorption of Al3+ on CDs-SCH hydrogel was limited by the monolayer coverage. In summary, the adsorption of Al3+ on CDs-SCH hydrogels involves not only the chelation of functional groups with Al3+, but also the encapsulation of Al3+ in the hydrogel. As far as the current reports are concerned, efficient adsorption of Cr6+, Fe3+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ has been achieved based on hydrogel materials (see Table 1 ), but few adsorption studies on Al3+ have been reported. This study provides a strategy for qualitative detection and removal of Al3+, contributing to the reduction of pressure in the fields of environmental pollution detection, environmental remediation and water treatment.

Here, a novel CDs-doped hydrogel with excellent optical properties was designed and synthesized. The CDs-SCH hydrogel enabled qualitative and quantitative characterization of pH changes, which could be used as an indicator of shrimp freshness. Al3+ and Ag+ effectively enhanced and burst the fluorescence of the CDs-SCH hydrogel with an acceptable detection limit and linear range. It can also be used as a reversible sensing platform for highly sensitive sensing of Ag+ and GSH, and this novel switching sensing strategy will be an advantageous tool for constructing dual sensing platforms. In addition, the adsorption performance of CDs-SCH hydrogel for Al3+ was significantly higher than that of SCH hydrogels. The adsorption process of metal ions was consistent with pseudo-second order kinetics and Langmuir model. The CDs-SCH hydrogel has a broad application prospect in the indication of freshness of aquatic products and the removal of ions detection by its high selectivity, low detection limit and fast response.
